title: Chapter 1
content:
# CHAPTER ONE: The Mirror Shows Nothing

The streetlights along Renmin South Road bled orange onto wet asphalt. Two in the morning, and Chengdu's late-summer humidity clung to everything like a wet sheet. Old Ma's taxi hummed at a red light, the only car in sight.

He'd been driving this route for thirty years. Knew which noodle shops stayed open past midnight, which alleys held the best street vendors, which corners attracted drunk college students who'd puke in his backseat and then argue about the fare. He knew the city the way old men know their own wives—not always pretty, but familiar down to the last wrinkle.

The light turned green.

He drove.

Three blocks later, a figure stepped off the curb. Not into the street—just to the edge, like they were waiting for a bus that wasn't coming. Old Ma pulled over without thinking. The figure wore a long coat, despite the heat, and moved with the careful gait of someone who hadn't slept in days.

The back door opened.

"Renmin Park," the passenger said. Male voice, young, but hollowed out. "The east gate."

Old Ma nodded and hit the meter. He pulled back into traffic, eyes automatically scanning the rearview mirror.

Nothing.

The back seat was empty.

Old Ma's hands stayed steady on the wheel. He'd been driving night shift long enough to know the difference between a trick of the light and something else entirely. Streetlights played games with shadows, sure. But they didn't erase a full-grown man from a mirror.

"I said Renmin Park," the voice repeated. Slightly annoyed now. "You deaf or just stupid?"

"Neither," Old Ma said. "Just checking."

He didn't check again. He didn't need to. The air in the taxi had changed—that was the first sign. It got heavier, like the passenger was pulling oxygen out of the space around him. The second sign was the smell. Wet earth and incense smoke, the kind that hung around old temples and freshly dug graves.

"You know," the passenger said, "most drivers would have freaked out by now."

"Most drivers don't work the night shift in Chengdu for thirty years."

A pause. Then a dry laugh that didn't carry any warmth. "Fair point."

They passed under a bridge. Graffiti covered the concrete walls, layer upon layer of tags and characters, some so old they'd faded into abstract shapes. A homeless man slept on a cardboard mat, his body curled around a half-empty bottle of baijiu.

"How much do you know?" the passenger asked.

"About what?"

"About what I am. About what this taxi is."

Old Ma took a left turn, his tires humming against the asphalt. "I know the meter works the same whether you've got a body or not. I know the fare's the same. And I know you'll get out when we get where you're going."

"That's it?"

"That's all I need."

The passenger laughed again, and this time there was something like respect in it. "You're a strange one, old man."

"People say that."

They drove in silence for a while. The streets grew narrower, older. Buildings pressed in from both sides, their balconies cluttered with laundry and potted plants and the occasional caged bird. Somewhere a dog started barking, then stopped.

Old Ma glanced at the meter. Fourteen yuan. Not much of a fare, but the night was young. And he'd learned long ago that passengers like this one didn't just appear by accident.

"You're going to ask," the passenger said. "They always ask."

"Ask what?"

"Why I'm dead. How I died. What unfinished business I've got dragging me back to the living world." The voice turned bitter. "The usual."

"I wasn't going to ask."

"Bullshit."

Old Ma shrugged. "I've been driving spirits for longer than you've been dead, kid. I've heard every story. Every tragedy. Every regret. You think you're special because you died young?"

Silence.

"I was twenty-three," the passenger finally said. "Car accident. Drunk driver. He walked away with a scratch."

"You want revenge?"

"I want—" The voice cut off. Then, softer: "I don't know what I want. That's the problem."

The taxi passed through another intersection. Street vendors had set up on the corner, selling grilled skewers and beer to the last of the night owls. The smoke from their grills curled upward, mixing with the humid air, and Old Ma caught the passenger inhaling deeply.

"You miss it," Old Ma said. "Living."

"Every second."

"Then why are you still here?"

The question hung in the air. The passenger didn't answer. Instead, he said: "Turn left up ahead."

Old Ma obeyed. They entered a residential district, the kind of place where old women still sat on stools outside their doors, fanning themselves and gossiping until the early hours. A few lights were on in the windows. Most were dark.

"There," the passenger said. "That building. The one with the red door."

Old Ma pulled over. The building was a typical Chengdu walk-up—five stories, no elevator, bamboo scaffolding covering one side where someone was doing renovations. A single light burned on the fourth floor.

"That's my mother's apartment," the passenger said. "She still leaves the light on for me. Every night. Even though she knows I'm not coming back."

Old Ma watched the glow through his windshield. "You want to go up?"

"No." The voice was barely a whisper. "I can't. I've tried. Every night for three months, I've tried to walk through that door. And every night, something stops me."

"Something?"

"I don't know what. A wall. A force. Like the building itself won't let me in."

Old Ma turned in his seat. He still couldn't see the passenger in the mirror, but he could feel his presence—a cold spot in the backseat, a weight that pressed against the upholstery.

"Three months," Old Ma said. "You've been wandering for three months?"

"I was waiting for you."

The words hit like a punch to the chest. Old Ma had suspected, of course. You didn't drive a spirit taxi for three decades without learning how the game worked. Certain passengers weren't random. Certain fares came with strings attached.

"Who sent you?" he asked.

"I don't know. A voice. In the dark. It told me to find the old taxi with the cracked windshield. Said you'd help me get home."

Old Ma's eyes drifted to the crack in his windshield. A spiderweb fracture in the lower right corner, from a stone that had kicked up on the highway five years ago. He'd never bothered to fix it. Bad luck to replace a windshield that had saved your life, his grandmother used to say.

"I'm not sure I can help you," he said.

"You're my only chance."

Old Ma sighed. He killed the engine and stepped out of the taxi. The night air hit him, still thick with humidity, but colder now. The kind of cold that settled in your bones and didn't leave.

The back door opened. Closed. The passenger was standing on the sidewalk now—a young man in a dark coat, pale as paper, with eyes that didn't quite catch the light.

"You can see me," the passenger said.

"I've always been able to see."

"Then you know."

Old Ma nodded. He knew. The dead didn't just get stuck by accident. Something was holding this boy here—something stronger than love, stronger than grief. And if he couldn't get through that red door, it meant whatever was holding him wasn't on the other side.

It was inside him.

"Tell me what happened that night," Old Ma said. "The whole truth. Not the version you've been telling yourself."

The passenger's face twisted. "I already told you. Drunk driver. Car accident. I died."

"No." Old Ma shook his head. "You said you were twenty-three. You said you died in a car accident. But you didn't say you were driving."

The passenger's eyes went wide.

"You didn't say you were the one behind the wheel."

The street went silent. Even the crickets stopped chirping. The passenger stood frozen, his mouth open, his hands shaking at his sides.

"I—" He stopped. Swallowed. "I don't remember."

"Yes you do."

"I don't—"

"Yes. You. Do."

The passenger crumpled. He sank to his knees on the wet sidewalk, his head in his hands, and for a long moment he didn't move. Then, in a voice that sounded like breaking glass:

"I was drunk. I got behind the wheel. I killed myself and I almost killed my best friend and I don't know how to face my mother because she raised me better than this and I—"

He broke off. Sobbing.

Old Ma stood over him, hands in his pockets. The night was cold and the street was empty and somewhere above them, a fourth-floor light burned for a son who would never come home.

"Your mother already knows," Old Ma said quietly. "She's known since the moment the police knocked on her door. She doesn't care how you died. She only cares that you're gone."

"But I can't—"

"You can. You just don't want to. There's a difference."

The passenger looked up. His face was streaked with tears that didn't quite look real—more like the memory of tears, the shape of grief without the substance.

"What do I do?"

Old Ma reached into his pocket and pulled out a worn brass key. Simple. Old-fashioned. The kind that unlocked nothing in the modern world.

"Go through the door," he said. "It'll open this time."

"How do you know?"

"Because I'm giving you permission. Your guilt locked you out. But guilt is just a story you tell yourself. And stories can be rewritten."

He held out the key. The passenger stared at it, then took it with trembling fingers.

"This isn't real," he whispered.

"It's as real as you need it to be."

The passenger stood. He looked at the key in his hand, then at the red door across the street. The light still burned on the fourth floor. The window was open, and Old Ma could see a silhouette moving inside—a woman, small and bent, making tea at two in the morning because she couldn't sleep.

"Thank you," the passenger said.

"Don't thank me yet. You still have to knock."

The passenger smiled. It was a thin, fragile thing, but it was real.

Then he walked across the street, pushed open the red door, and disappeared inside.

Old Ma stood alone on the sidewalk. The light on the fourth floor flickered once. Twice.

Then it went dark.

He got back in his taxi and started the engine. The meter had reset itself. The back seat was empty. The smell of incense was fading.

He drove.

Three blocks later, his phone buzzed. A text from an unknown number:

*New fare. Jinli Ancient Street. 3am. She'll be wearing red.*

Old Ma stared at the screen for a long time.

Then he put the phone away and kept driving.

The night was still young.

title: The Woman Who Didn't Blink
content:
The digital clock on the dashboard read 2:47 AM when Old Ma pulled into the Jinniu District petrol station. His bones ached. The night had been slow — three fares since midnight, all living, all drunk, all smelling like a distillery had vomited on them.

He killed the engine and sat in the silence. The station lights buzzed overhead, casting everything in that sickly orange glow that made ghosts of the living.

The bell above the station door chimed. An old man in a grease-stained vest shuffled out, cigarette dangling from his lips. He tapped on the driver's window.

"Ma Ge." He grinned, showing yellow teeth. "Long time no see."

Old Ma rolled down the window. "Lao Xu. You still working this graveyard shift?"

"Someone has to serve the night owls." Lao Xu squinted at the taxi. "You look like shit."

"I feel like shit."

"Want me to check your oil? Your tires look low."

Old Ma shook his head. "Just fill her up. Regular."

Lao Xu grabbed the nozzle and popped the fuel cap. The pump clicked and hummed. "Heard about that ghost bus incident on the news. Three students. Hit a tree on Erhuan Road."

"I know."

"They say the driver saw something on the road. A woman in white."

Old Ma said nothing.

Lao Xu finished filling, capped the tank, and leaned against the door. "Ma Ge. You've been driving this city for thirty years. You ever seen something... strange?"

"You believe in ghosts, Lao Xu?"

The old man laughed, but it didn't reach his eyes. "I believe in what I see. And I've seen you pick up passengers at this station at 3 AM. Every time. Like clockwork."

Old Ma handed him cash. "Keep the change."

"Ma Ge — "

"Goodnight, Lao Xu."

He started the engine and pulled out. In his rearview mirror, he watched Lao Xu standing there, watching him go, the cigarette smoke curling around his head like a question mark.

The taxi hummed along Renmin South Road. Streetlights flickered. The city slept behind closed curtains, behind locked doors, behind lives that didn't include driving through the dark alone.

Old Ma checked his phone. No messages. No calls.

Three weeks since the girl in the white dress. Three weeks since he'd started asking questions nobody wanted to answer.

He'd visited the archives at the old city records office. He'd called in favors with retired cops who owed him. He'd traced the history of that stretch of Erhuan Road where the bus had crashed.

And what he'd found made his blood run cold.

Seven accidents at that exact location in the past fifteen years. Seven. All involving vehicles that carried multiple passengers. All happening between 3:10 and 3:30 AM. All with no explanation — no mechanical failure, no drunk drivers, no debris on the road.

Just drivers who said the same thing: "I saw a woman in white."

And then the official reports would change. The witnesses would recant. The files would be sealed.

Someone was cleaning up the mess. Someone with power. Someone who didn't want people looking too close.

Old Ma's hands tightened on the wheel.

His phone buzzed.

He glanced at it. Unknown number. Message preview: *Pick up passenger. Wuhou Temple. East gate. 3:15 AM.*

He knew better than to ask who was sending these messages. He'd tried. The number always came back as disconnected.

But the fares always came.

He checked the clock. 3:05 AM. Ten minutes.

He turned onto Wuhou Street. The trees along the road threw shadows like reaching hands. The temple complex loomed ahead, its red walls and curved roofs dark against the night sky.

The east gate was closed. Locked. Had been since 8 PM.

Old Ma pulled over and killed the engine. He waited.

The minutes crawled.

3:13. 3:14. 3:15.

Nothing.

He checked his phone. No new messages. No passenger waiting at the gate.

For a moment, he considered driving off. Maybe it was a joke. Maybe whoever was sending these messages had finally slipped up.

Then he saw her.

She was standing at the gate. Right at the gate, as if she'd been there the whole time and he'd just missed her. A woman in her late twenties, maybe early thirties. Plain black dress. Hair pulled back. No makeup. No expression.

She didn't blink.

Old Ma watched her for a full thirty seconds. She stared back. Not moving. Not adjusting her position. Not breathing — at least, not that he could see.

He reached over and unlocked the back door.

She opened it, slid in, and closed it with a soft click. No sound of fabric rustling. No scent of perfume or sweat or anything human.

"Where to?" Old Ma asked.

She gave him an address in the northern suburbs. A residential district called Shuiniuhe. Water Buffalo River.

"That's a long drive."

She said nothing.

Old Ma put the car in gear and pulled away. In the rearview mirror, he watched her. She sat perfectly still, hands folded in her lap, eyes fixed on the back of his head.

"You know," he said, trying to keep his voice light, "most passengers tell me where they're going without me asking."

Still nothing.

"Okay. Silent type. I get it. I've driven plenty of those."

The taxi moved through the empty streets. The city lights grew sparse as they headed north. Old Ma's hands were steady on the wheel, but his mind was racing.

Something was wrong with this one. Different from the others.

The girl in the white dress had been desperate. Afraid. Human.

This woman was... empty.

"Why did you call me?" she said suddenly.

Her voice was flat. No emotion. Like a recording.

Old Ma's eyes flicked to the rearview mirror. "I didn't call you. I got a message to pick up a passenger."

"Who sent the message?"

"I don't know."

"You're lying."

The words hit him like cold water. "Excuse me?"

"You're lying, Chen Mo."

His name. She knew his name.

He gripped the wheel tighter. "How do you know my name?"

"I know many things about you, Chen Mo. I know you've been asking questions about the accidents on Erhuan Road. I know you've been visiting the archives. I know you've been talking to retired officers."

Old Ma's blood went cold. "Who are you?"

"I'm the one who's been sending you passengers."

The words hung in the air. The taxi hummed. The streetlights flashed by.

"You," Old Ma said slowly. "You're the one."

"Yes."

"Why?"

"Because I need your help."

"I don't understand."

The woman leaned forward. Her face appeared in the rearview mirror, inches from his. Her eyes were dark. Empty. Like holes punched into a mask.

"There's something under Chengdu, Chen Mo. Something old. Something that's been sleeping for a long time. And it's waking up."

Old Ma's hands trembled. "What are you talking about?"

"The accidents on Erhuan Road. The ghosts you've been driving. The spirits that gather at certain intersections, at certain times, drawn by something they can't name." Her voice dropped. "They're not random. They're symptoms. Signs of a sickness spreading through this city."

"Sickness?"

"There's a crack in the boundary between the living world and the spirit realm. Right beneath Chengdu. And something is pushing through."

Old Ma shook his head. "This is insane. I'm a taxi driver. I drive ghosts. That's it. I'm not some — "

"You're more than a taxi driver, Chen Mo. You've always been more. That's why I chose you."

"Chose me for what?"

The woman's eyes flickered. For the first time, something like emotion crossed her face. Fear.

"To stop what's coming."

The taxi hit a bump. Old Ma's eyes shot to the road — and he slammed on the brakes.

A woman was standing in the middle of the street.

She was wearing a white dress.

The same white dress.

The taxi screeched to a halt, inches from her. The headlights illuminated her pale face, her hollow eyes, her open mouth.

"I found her," the woman in the back seat whispered. "I finally found her."

Old Ma's heart hammered. "That's — that's the ghost from Erhuan Road."

"Yes."

"Who is she?"

"She's the first one. The one who started it all."

The woman in white turned her head. Her eyes met Old Ma's through the windshield.

And she smiled.

The taxi's engine died. The lights flickered. The temperature dropped so fast Old Ma could see his breath.

"Chen Mo," the woman behind him said. "Whatever you do — don't get out of the car."

The woman in white started walking toward them.

"Chen Mo."

Her feet didn't touch the ground.

"CHEN MO."

The driver's door opened.

Old Ma looked down.

There was no one there. No hand on the handle. No force pulling it.

The door just opened.

The woman in white stood beside it. She reached inside. Her fingers brushed his arm.

Ice. Pure, absolute ice.

"Hello, taxi driver," she said. "I've been waiting for you."

And the world went black.

title: The Woman Who Forgot Her Name
content:
The rain came down hard over Chengdu that night, turning the neon lights into bleeding streaks of color across the windshield. Old Ma pulled his taxi into the waiting area outside Chunxi Road's shopping district, killed the engine, and lit a cigarette.

Three hours until midnight. Three hours of regular fares before the real work began.

He watched a group of drunk office workers stumble out of a hotpot restaurant, laughing too loud, arms around each other's shoulders. One of them, a young woman in a red dress, broke away from the group and walked toward his taxi. She moved differently than her companions—steady, deliberate, like she was walking through water.

Old Ma took a long drag of his cigarette and watched her.

She opened the back door and slid in without meeting his eyes through the window. The door closed with a solid thud.

"Where to?" he asked, not turning around.

"Lotus Hill Cemetery." Her voice was soft, almost lost in the drumming rain. "Gate three."

Old Ma's fingers tightened on the steering wheel. Lotus Hill was twenty kilometers outside the city. Gate three was the oldest section, the part that dated back to the Qing Dynasty.

"Do you know what time it is?" he asked.

"Yes." She paused. "I know what time it is."

He crushed his cigarette in the ashtray and started the engine. The taxi pulled away from the curb, its headlights cutting through the sheets of rain.

They drove in silence for ten minutes. The woman in the back seat stared out the window, watching the city lights blur past. Old Ma watched her in the rearview mirror.

She was young—maybe twenty-five, maybe younger. Her red dress was simple but elegant, the kind of thing a woman wore to a wedding or a funeral. Her hair was pulled back, revealing a face that might have been beautiful if it hadn't been so empty.

But it was her hands that told him everything. They lay perfectly still in her lap, palms up, fingers slightly curled. The hands of someone who no longer had any tension in their body.

"You don't remember, do you?" Old Ma said quietly.

Her eyes met his in the mirror. "Remember what?"

"How you got in my taxi."

She was quiet for a long time. The windshield wipers beat a steady rhythm against the glass.

"I was at a restaurant," she finally said. "With friends. We were celebrating. My friend Li Na—she just got promoted. We had hotpot and baijiu. And then I needed to go home. So I walked outside and I saw your taxi."

"And before that?"

"Before that?"

"Before the restaurant. What's your name?"

She opened her mouth. Closed it. Her brow furrowed.

"I... I don't..."

"What street did you grow up on?"

She shook her head slowly, like she was trying to clear fog from her mind.

"What's your mother's name?"

The woman's hands began to tremble. "I don't know. I don't remember. Why can't I remember?"

Old Ma pulled the taxi over to the side of the road. He turned off the engine and twisted in his seat to face her fully.

"You're dead," he said.

The words hung in the air between them.

"No," she whispered. "That's not possible. I was just at dinner. I could taste the food. I could feel the rain on my skin when I walked to your car."

"Feel this." Old Ma reached into his pocket and pulled out a lighter. He flicked it on, holding the small flame in front of her.

She reached out her hand toward the fire. The flame didn't flicker. Her fingers passed through it like smoke.

She stared at her hand. Then she looked at Old Ma with eyes that were suddenly very clear.

"How did I die?"

"I don't know yet. That's your job to figure out."

"But you drive spirits. Everyone in Chengdu who knows about these things knows about you. Old Ma and his Spirit Taxi. You're supposed to help us."

"I can help you cross over. But you have to remember what happened. You have to find your unfinished business."

She pressed her palms against her temples. "I can't remember anything. Just the restaurant. Just the rain. Just walking to your car."

Old Ma studied her for a long moment. Then he started the engine again.

"Where are we going?" she asked.

"To find your memory. Tell me what you do remember. Anything."

She closed her eyes, concentrating. "The restaurant. It was on Kehua North Road. The one with the red lanterns outside. We were on the second floor. Li Na was crying because she was happy. She kept refilling my cup with baijiu."

"How many cups?"

"I don't know. Maybe four. Maybe five."

"Then what?"

"Then I needed to use the bathroom. I stood up. I walked toward the stairs to the first floor. And then..." Her voice trailed off.

"And then?"

"Nothing. I don't remember anything until I was standing in the rain, looking at your taxi."

Old Ma turned the taxi around and headed back toward the city center. The rain had begun to ease, turning into a fine mist that clung to the windows.

"Here's what we're going to do," he said. "We're going back to the restaurant. You're going to walk through that door and you're going to remember what happened after you left the table."

"I can't just walk into a restaurant like this. No one will be able to see me."

"They won't see you. But you'll see them. You'll see the place. And maybe that'll be enough to jog your memory."

They drove in silence back to Kehua North Road. The restaurant was still open, its red lanterns glowing in the mist. A few tables were occupied, late-night diners picking at cold dishes.

"Go," Old Ma said. "I'll wait here."

She looked at him with something like fear in her eyes. "What if I can't remember?"

"Then you'll be stuck in this world until your spirit fades into nothing. You'll wander the streets, watching the living go about their lives, until one day you just stop existing."

"That's a horrible thing to say."

"It's the truth. You wanted a spirit taxi driver? I don't sugarcoat things."

She took a deep breath that she didn't need and opened the door. She stepped out into the mist and walked toward the restaurant. Old Ma watched her pass through the front door like she was walking through a curtain of water.

He lit another cigarette and waited.

Ten minutes passed. Twenty. The cigarette burned down to the filter. He lit another one.

Thirty minutes.

He was starting to get worried when the passenger door opened and the woman slid into the front seat beside him.

"I remember," she said. Her voice was strange. Hollow.

"What happened?"

"I went to the bathroom. I was dizzy from the baijiu. I walked down the stairs and I missed the last step. I fell." She touched her neck. "I broke my neck. I died right there in the stairwell. Li Na must have found me. She must have called an ambulance. But I was already gone."

Old Ma nodded slowly. "Then what? Why are you still here? What's your unfinished business?"

She turned to face him, and for the first time since she'd entered his taxi, she smiled. It was not a pleasant smile.

"My name is Chen Xiaoling," she said. "And I was murdered."

Old Ma's hand stopped halfway to his cigarette.

"Murdered? You said you fell."

"That's what they'll rule it as. That's what it looked like. But I didn't just miss a step. Someone pushed me."

"Who?"

"I don't know. I didn't see their face. But I felt their hands on my back. Two hands. Pushing me forward. Hard enough that I flew down those stairs like a rag doll."

Old Ma studied her face in the dim light of the taxi. "Are you sure? Memory can play tricks on the dead. Fear can reshape what happened."

"I'm sure." Her eyes were hard now. "I felt their hands. I felt the fabric of their sleeves brush against my skin. I remember the smell of their perfume."

"Perfume?"

"Something floral. Jasmine, maybe. Or gardenia. It was the last thing I smelled before I hit the ground."

Old Ma was quiet for a long moment. Then he reached into the glove compartment and pulled out a worn leather notebook. He flipped to a fresh page and clicked his pen.

"Tell me everything about that night. Everyone you saw. Everyone you talked to. Every detail, no matter how small."

She talked for an hour. About Li Na and her promotion. About the other friends at the table. About the baijiu and the laughter and the red lanterns. About the walk to the bathroom and the hands on her back and the smell of jasmine.

Old Ma wrote it all down. Then he closed his notebook.

"I'll look into it," he said. "But you need to understand something. I drive spirits across. I find their unfinished business and I help them cross over. I'm not a detective. I'm not a revenge service."

"I don't want revenge. I want justice."

"What's the difference?"

She didn't have an answer for that.

Old Ma started the engine. "I know a place. A temple on the outskirts of the city. The monks there maintain a spirit gate. You'll be safe there until I find out what happened."

"And if you don't find out?"

"Then you'll be in the temple until your spirit fades. It's a better fate than wandering the streets."

He drove through the misty streets of Chengdu, past the closed shops and empty parks, past the street vendors packing up their stalls, past the night markets where the last die-hard customers haggled over prices. The woman beside him sat in silence, staring at her hands.

They reached the temple just before midnight. It was a small building, tucked between two modern apartment blocks, its roof curving up toward the sky like a bird taking flight. Old Ma parked the taxi and walked her to the gate.

A young monk appeared, his bald head glistening in the moonlight. He looked at the woman, then at Old Ma.

"Another one?" the monk asked.

"Another one."

"You know there's a limit to how many we can shelter here."

"I know." Old Ma reached into his pocket and pulled out a stack of cash. "This should cover her stay."

The monk took the money without counting it. "She's the third one this month."

"I know."

"Someone's been sending them to you."

"I know."

"Doesn't that strike you as strange? Three spirits in one month, all of them recent deaths, all of them with unfinished business. That's not normal. Even for you."

Old Ma looked at the woman—Chen Xiaoling—standing in the temple courtyard, staring up at the moon.

"It strikes me as very strange," he said.

The monk followed his gaze. "There's something else."

"What?"

"Before she arrived, I had a vision during meditation. A shadow moving through the city. Someone who walks between worlds like you do. But darker. Older."

Old Ma turned to face the monk fully. "Did you see their face?"

"No." The monk's voice was barely a whisper. "But I saw what they were carrying. A list. A long list of names."

The woman turned to look at them, her face pale in the moonlight.

The monk pointed at her.

"Hers was on it."

Old Ma felt something cold settle in his chest. He looked at Chen Xiaoling, then back at the monk.

"How many names?"

The monk's face was grim.

"Enough to fill a hundred taxis."

The wind picked up,

title: The Passenger Who Knew His Name
content:
The clock on the dashboard read 1:47 AM when Old Ma pulled into the Sichuan University west gate. The street was empty — not a student in sight, not even the usual noodle cart that set up near the dormitories. The air smelled of rain that hadn't fallen yet.

He killed the engine and waited.

Thirty years on this job, and he'd learned to trust the silence. When the streets went quiet like this, when even the stray dogs found somewhere else to be, it meant a fare was coming. Not the kind that paid in cash.

The back door opened.

Old Ma didn't flinch. He adjusted his rearview mirror and saw a young woman in her early twenties, wearing a university tracksuit with the SU logo stitched on the chest. Her hair was wet, plastered to her face. Dripping onto the vinyl seats.

"Where to?" he asked.

She didn't answer. Just stared at him through the mirror with eyes that were too still, too dark.

Old Ma sighed. "Miss, I need an address. Or at least a direction."

"The hospital," she said. Her voice sounded like paper being torn. "West China Hospital. The morgue."

He put the car in gear. "That's a long ride for this time of night."

"I know."

The taxi pulled away from the curb. Old Ma watched her in the mirror as they turned onto Renmin South Road. She sat perfectly still, hands folded in her lap, water pooling around her on the seat. A lake student, he guessed. Drowned, maybe. He'd seen enough wet ghosts to recognize the signs.

"Cold back there?" he asked. "I can turn up the heat."

"I don't feel cold anymore."

"Right. Stupid question."

They drove in silence for three blocks. The streetlights flickered as they passed, casting long shadows that seemed to reach for the taxi. Old Ma kept his eyes on the road. He'd learned long ago that staring at ghosts made them nervous. Made them remember things they'd rather forget.

"I know who you are," the girl said suddenly.

Old Ma's hands tightened on the wheel. "Is that so?"

"You're Old Ma. The Spirit Taxi driver. They say you've been ferrying souls for thirty years."

"They say a lot of things."

"They say you're the only one who can see us. The only one who will take us where we need to go."

He didn't answer. The traffic light ahead turned red, and he stopped, even though no other cars were on the road. Some habits couldn't be broken, even for ghosts.

"I've been looking for you," she continued. "I died three weeks ago. Drowned in the lake. They found my body on a Tuesday morning. The funeral was last Friday. My parents flew in from Harbin. My mother cried so hard she couldn't stand."

Old Ma watched the light. Red. Still red.

"I know all this," he said quietly. "I can feel it. You don't need to tell me."

"But I need to tell someone. That's why I'm here. That's why I found you."

The light turned green. He pressed the accelerator.

"Someone sent me," she said. "A man. He came to me while I was still in the water. Before they found me. He told me to wait. Told me you would come."

Old Ma's blood went cold. Three weeks. She'd been waiting in the lake for three weeks. That wasn't natural. Ghosts didn't linger that long unless something held them there. Unless someone held them there.

"What did he look like?"

"I couldn't see his face. He wore a hat. A black hat with a wide brim. But his voice — it was like stones grinding together. He said you would know what to do."

The taxi lurched. Old Ma had pressed the brake without meaning to. They were stopped in the middle of the intersection, surrounded by darkness, the streetlights flickering like dying candles.

"He said you would take me to the hospital," the girl continued. "He said you would help me find my body."

"Your body?"

"They performed an autopsy. They said it was an accident. But it wasn't. I didn't fall into that lake. I was pushed."

Old Ma turned around to face her. A mistake. He knew it the moment he saw her face — pale as rice paper, eyes black as ink, mouth open in a silent scream that she'd been holding for three weeks.

"Who pushed you?"

"I don't know. I was walking back from the library. It was dark. Someone grabbed my arm. I remember the water. I remember struggling. I remember — "

She stopped. Her hands flew to her throat.

"I can't breathe," she whispered.

"You're dead," Old Ma said gently. "You don't need to breathe."

"I know. But I can't — I can't stop — "

Her body convulsed. Water poured from her mouth, her nose, her eyes — gallons of it, flooding the back seat, soaking the floor mats, running in rivers down the windows. Old Ma grabbed a rag from the glove compartment and pressed it to his face.

"Stop," he said. "You're not in the water anymore."

"I can't — "

"Listen to me. You're not in the water. You're in my taxi. You're safe."

She kept convulsing. The water kept coming. Old Ma reached back and grabbed her wrist. Her skin was cold as the lake bottom.

"I said stop."

She went still. The water stopped. The taxi was drenched, but the girl sat perfectly calm now, her face composed, her hands folded in her lap.

"I'm sorry," she said. "I can't control it. When I remember — "

"I know. It's okay."

He released her wrist and turned back to the wheel. His hand was shaking. He didn't want her to see.

They drove the rest of the way in silence. The hospital appeared ahead, a massive complex of buildings that glowed like a beacon in the darkness. West China Hospital. One of the biggest in the province. Old Ma had been here a hundred times. Maybe a thousand. Every ghost in Chengdu seemed to end up here eventually.

He pulled into the parking lot and stopped near the morgue entrance. The building was dark. No lights on. The morgue staff wouldn't arrive until morning.

"This is it," he said. "But I don't know what you expect to find. Your body is in a refrigeration drawer. You can't — "

"I need to see it."

"Miss — "

"Please. I need to see it. I need to see what they did to me."

Old Ma looked at her in the mirror. Her eyes were still black, but there was something else in them now. Something desperate. Something human.

"Wait here," he said.

He got out of the taxi and walked to the morgue entrance. The door was locked, of course. But Old Ma had been doing this for thirty years. He knew the tricks.

He pulled a key from his pocket. Not a real key — a spirit key. It looked like something from a child's toy, made of cheap brass, but it could open any door in the city. The ghosts had given it to him, years ago. He didn't ask where they got it.

The lock clicked. The door swung open.

The morgue was cold and silent. Fluorescent lights hummed overhead, casting everything in a pale green glow. Old Ma walked past the rows of stainless steel tables, past the sinks and the scales and the instruments laid out on trays, until he reached the refrigeration unit.

Drawer 47. That's what the girl had shown him. He pulled it open.

She was beautiful. Even in death, even pale and cold and covered with a thin layer of frost, she was beautiful. Her hair was spread around her head like a dark halo. Her eyes were closed. Her lips were blue.

Old Ma stood there for a long moment, looking at her.

Then he heard footsteps behind him.

He turned. The girl was standing in the doorway of the morgue, dripping water onto the tile floor. She was looking past him, at her own body.

"Oh," she said. "Oh, I see."

"What do you see?"

"I see everything. I see the moment he pushed me. I see his hands on my back. I see his face."

She turned to Old Ma. Her eyes were no longer black. They were clear. Human. Terrified.

"It was a policeman," she said. "He was wearing a uniform. He pushed me into the lake and he watched me drown and he didn't do anything to help."

Old Ma felt his stomach drop. "A policeman? You're sure?"

"I'm sure. I remember his face. I remember everything."

She walked to the drawer and looked down at her own body. Her hand reached out, almost touching the cold skin.

"I'm sorry," she said. "I'm sorry I couldn't be stronger. I'm sorry I couldn't fight."

"You're dead," Old Ma said. "You don't need to be sorry."

"But I do. Because I know something else. Something he told me while I was drowning."

She looked up at him. Her eyes were filling with tears. Or maybe it was just the lake water, still draining from her body.

"He said to tell you that this is only the beginning. He said to tell you that you've been driving the wrong passengers. That the ones who really matter — the ones who are coming for you — they haven't arrived yet."

Old Ma's hands went cold. "Who said this? The policeman?"

"No. The man in the black hat. He was standing on the shore, watching. He didn't try to save me. He just watched. And when I was gone, he smiled."

The fluorescent lights flickered. The temperature in the room dropped. Old Ma could see his breath now, white clouds forming in the air.

"Where is he?" he asked. "Where can I find him?"

"I don't know. But he told me to give you a message."

She stepped closer. Her voice dropped to a whisper.

"He said to tell you that he knows your real name. He said to tell you that he knows what you did in 1989. He said that if you want to keep your secrets, you'll do exactly what he says."

Old Ma felt the world tilt. 1989. Thirty years ago. Before the taxi. Before the ghosts. Before any of this.

"How does he know that?"

"I don't know. But he said you would understand. He said you would remember the girl in the red dress."

Old Ma couldn't breathe. The morgue walls pressed in on him. The fluorescent lights buzzed like angry insects.

The girl in the red dress. He'd never told anyone about her. Not a single soul in thirty years.

"How — " he started.

But when he looked up, the ghost was gone. The drawer was empty. The body had vanished.

Only one thing remained.

A single red dress, folded neatly on the stainless steel table.

title: Chapter 5
content:
CHAPTER 5: THE WOMAN IN THE BACK SEAT

The rain came down hard over Chengdu, turning the streets into rivers of reflected neon. Old Ma pulled his taxi into the taxi stand outside Chunxi Road station, killed the engine, and lit a cigarette. The clock on his dashboard read 11:47 PM.

He'd been driving since three in the afternoon. His back ached. His eyes burned. But he couldn't go home yet.

Not after last night's fare.

The woman in the white dress. The way she'd vanished from his back seat before he even fully stopped the car. The cold spot that lingered on his leather upholstery for hours afterward.

He took a long drag and watched the rain streak across his windshield.

Thirty years in this business. Thirty years of night shifts, strange passengers, and things that shouldn't exist. He'd learned to recognize them by now. The way they didn't cast shadows under streetlights. The way their breath never fogged his windows in winter. The way they always paid with old bills, wrinkled and smelling of camphor wood.

But this one was different.

She'd gotten in at the usual spot — the corner of Kehua North Road, where the old apartment buildings cast long shadows across the sidewalk. She didn't speak. Didn't give him an address. Just sat in the back seat, hands folded in her lap, staring straight ahead.

He'd asked where she wanted to go.

"Just drive," she'd said. "I'll know when we get there."

They drove for forty minutes. Through the tunnel under Renmin South Road, past the lights of Tianfu Square, into the older neighborhoods where the buildings had moss growing on their walls and the street vendors sold stinky tofu from carts with squeaky wheels.

She finally spoke when they reached Jinli Old Street.

"Stop here."

He pulled over. Checked his rearview mirror.

The back seat was empty.

No door had opened. No shadow had moved. She was just gone, like smoke through a cracked window.

Old Ma crushed out his cigarette in the ashtray. The rain was letting up. Time to make some real money before shift ended.

He turned the key.

The engine coughed. Sputtered. Died.

"Come on." He tried again. Nothing. The battery light flickered weakly, then went dark.

He checked his watch. Five minutes to midnight.

A knock on his passenger window made him jump.

Old Ma turned. A woman stood outside in the rain, holding a black umbrella. She was young, maybe late twenties, with sharp features and wet hair plastered to her forehead. She wore a gray business suit that looked expensive, even soaked through.

She gestured for him to roll down the window.

He pressed the button. The window lowered with a groan.

"Are you available?" she asked.

"Car's dead."

"I saw you trying to start it." She smiled, but it didn't reach her eyes. "Maybe I can help. I know a bit about engines."

Old Ma studied her. Something about her voice was familiar. The way she held herself, too still, too controlled.

"Who are you?"

"My name is Lin. I need a ride. It's urgent."

"To where?"

"The Second People's Hospital. East wing. The morgue."

The rain picked up again. Fat drops hammered against her umbrella, creating a rhythm like drumming fingers.

Old Ma's hand went to the door handle. "Get in."

The car started on his first try.

Lin folded her umbrella and slid into the passenger seat. The smell of wet fabric filled the cab, mixed with something else. Something chemical. Antiseptic.

"You're not a spirit," Old Ma said.

"No."

"Then how'd you know to find me here?"

She buckled her seatbelt. "Because you're the only one who can take me where I need to go."

He pulled away from the curb, tires splashing through puddles. The streets were empty now. No other cars. No pedestrians walking dogs or hurrying home from late shifts.

Just him and Lin and the rain.

"The morgue," he said. "That's an unusual destination for this time of night."

"My sister is there."

"I'm sorry."

"Don't be. She's not dead."

Old Ma's hands tightened on the steering wheel. "The morgue keeps only the dead."

"Usually. But my sister went in three days ago for an autopsy. The coroner was a friend of the family. He called me tonight, said something was wrong. That she moved."

The traffic light ahead turned red. Old Ma slowed to a stop, even though no other cars were around.

"People don't move in the morgue," he said.

"My sister isn't people. Not anymore." Lin reached into her jacket and pulled out a photograph. She held it up for him to see.

The woman in the photo had long black hair, delicate features, and eyes that seemed to look right through the camera. She wore a white dress.

The same white dress as his passenger from last night.

"Is this her?" Old Ma asked.

"You've seen her."

It wasn't a question.

"Last night. She got in my cab. We drove for a while. Then she disappeared."

Lin's face went pale. "Where did she get in?"

"Kehua North Road."

"My sister died on Kehua North Road. Hit by a delivery truck. She was crossing the street to buy medicine for our mother."

The light turned green. Old Ma pressed the accelerator, but the car didn't move. The engine revved, whined, then went silent.

"What's happening?" Lin asked.

"I don't know."

He tried the ignition. Nothing. The dashboard lights flickered once, twice, then went dark.

A cold draft swept through the car, even though all the windows were closed.

"She's here," Lin whispered.

Old Ma looked in the rearview mirror.

The woman in the white dress was sitting in the back seat.

Her skin was the color of moonlight. Her eyes were black, no pupils, no irises, just empty darkness. Her lips moved, but no sound came out.

"She says she's sorry," Old Ma said, surprising himself.

Lin turned around slowly. "Can you hear her?"

"I can feel her."

"You never told me you could do that."

"You never asked."

The woman in white raised her hand and pointed. Past Lin, past the windshield, to something on the road ahead.

Old Ma followed her gaze.

A figure stood in the middle of the intersection. Tall. Male. Wearing a long coat that seemed to absorb light. His face was hidden in shadow, but Old Ma could feel him staring at them.

"He's watching," Old Ma said.

"Who?"

"I don't know."

The woman in white opened her mouth, and this time, sound came out. A low moan that rose in pitch until it became a scream.

The windshield cracked. A spiderweb of fractures spread from the center, blurring the figure in the intersection.

"Make her stop!" Lin shouted.

"I can't."

The screaming continued. The car shook. The streetlights above them flickered and died, plunging them into darkness.

Then silence.

The woman in white was gone.

The figure in the intersection was gone.

The streetlights came back on, one by one, casting pools of yellow light on the wet asphalt.

Old Ma's hands were shaking. He gripped the steering wheel until his knuckles turned white.

"She's gone," Lin said, her voice hollow.

"For now."

"What did she want?"

"She was trying to warn us." Old Ma turned the key. The engine started smoothly, like nothing had happened. "About him."

"The man in the intersection."

"Yes."

Lin was quiet for a moment. When she spoke again, her voice was barely above a whisper. "I've seen him before. At the hospital. He was there the night my sister died. Standing in the parking lot, just watching."

"Why didn't you tell me?"

"Because I didn't know what he was. I still don't."

Old Ma put the car in gear and drove through the intersection. The hospital was only a few blocks away now. He could see its lights in the distance, red and white, blinking against the rain-scrubbed sky.

"I think he's the one sending the spirits to me," Old Ma said.

"Sending them?"

"Last night was the first time. But it's been happening more often now. Spirits flagging me down, getting in my cab, leading me somewhere specific. I thought it was coincidence. But it's not."

"What does he want?"

"I don't know. But I intend to find out."

He pulled into the hospital parking lot. The east wing was dark, all its windows black except for one on the third floor.

The morgue.

Lin got out of the car. The rain had stopped, leaving the air heavy and damp. She looked up at that single lit window.

"She's up there," Lin said. "Waiting for me."

"Be careful."

"Come with me."

Old Ma hesitated. He'd been to hospitals before. Never for good reasons. The dead always found him there, drawn to him like moths to flame.

"I don't think I can help."

"You already have." Lin reached into her pocket and pulled out a folded piece of paper. "Here. This is for you."

He took it. Unfolded it. Saw a hand-drawn map, the streets of Chengdu marked in careful detail. A red circle around the location of a building he didn't recognize.

"What is this?"

"The place where my sister worked. Before she died. She was a researcher. Studying something she wasn't supposed to find."

"Studying what?"

Lin looked at him. Her eyes were wet, but she wasn't crying.

"The boundary between life and death. She said there was a crack in it. A hole. And someone was using it to send things through."

Old Ma's blood went cold.

"The spirits."

"Yes. And worse." She pointed to the map. "Go there. You'll find answers."

She turned and walked toward the hospital entrance. Her footsteps echoed in the empty parking lot.

Old Ma watched her go. The door swung open, swallowed her inside, swung shut.

He looked down at the map in his hands.

The building was marked in red ink. A single word written beside it in Chinese characters:

归墟

The Abyss.

The car's engine idled behind him. The headlights cast long shadows across the wet pavement. And somewhere in the darkness, the man in the long coat was watching.

Old Ma folded the map, put it in his pocket, and got back in the taxi.

He had a new destination now.
